summary: Dan Shapiro responds to Microsoft Ventures head Rahul Sood's tweet calling Startup Weekend Women 'pointless.' Shapiro dismantles Sood's argument that drawing attention to gender separation perpetuates it, comparing this logic to ignoring Ebola and likening it to a child's lack of object permanence. He presents testimony from women entrepreneurs who credit women-specific events with launching their careers. The post argues that women-focused startup events are essential for widening the entrepreneurship funnel and that dismissing them dishonors the volunteers and organizers who make them possible.
key_points: Rahul Sood's claim that women-specific startup events are 'pointless' reflects a flawed 'ignore the problem and it goes away' philosophy
Women-specific events demonstrably draw participants who would not otherwise attend, expanding the pipeline into entrepreneurship
Same-sex role models are backed by research as effective motivators for women in STEM and entrepreneurship
Women who attend women-specific events often go on to participate in general events, improving diversity across the board
money_quotes: I have seen this perspective before: 'If we ignore the problem, it will go away'. I think it would work for sexism as well as it does for Ebola.
I wouldn't have started a company, had I not attended Startup Weekend Women's Edition.
If Startup Weekend helps people get towards the first step of entrepreneurship then that is exactly the place we should spend our efforts in increasing diversity.
Ask the women who became entrepreneurs because of the many women specific events if they were 'pointless'. I doubt it. And it is their opinion that matters here.
Rahul's comments on behalf of Microsoft Ventures dishonor them all.
key_insight: Women-specific startup events are not redundant but essential pipeline-builders, and dismissing them as pointless reflects a naive belief that ignoring systemic problems makes them disappear.
